one_liner: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the ED after a syncopal episode during swim team practice. She briefly lost consciousness while diving, and has had two similar events over the past year.

stage2.hpi_extension: Witnesses describe a sudden collapse with a few seconds of jerking that resolved spontaneously. She regained consciousness within a minute without confusion or incontinence. She reports a prior fainting episode while startled by an alarm clock and another during an argument. She denies drug use. A maternal uncle died suddenly at age 22 during sleep. She takes no QT-prolonging medications. She has always had normal hearing.
stage2.pmh: Previously healthy; prior syncope evaluations unrevealing
stage2.family_history: Maternal uncle with sudden cardiac death at age 22; unexplained drowning of a cousin at age 14
stage2.social_history: High school student and competitive swimmer. No tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: General: alert, well-appearing teenager. HEENT: normal; hearing grossly intact. Cardiovascular: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, no gallops. Lungs clear. Neuro: non-focal; no residual post-ictal confusion. Extremities: no edema. Tongue not bitten.

lab_results.result: Sinus rhythm at 64 bpm. QTc 510 ms (prolonged) with broad, notched T waves in precordial leads. No delta wave, no Brugada pattern. Telemetry captures a brief run of polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with twisting of the QRS axis around the baseline (torsades de pointes) that self-terminated.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A QTc exceeding 450 ms in males or 460 ms in females is prolonged; QTc >500 ms markedly increases the risk of torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death. Documented torsades confirms the arrhythmic substrate.

lab_results.result: Sodium 140, potassium 4.2, magnesium 2.0, calcium 9.4. Renal and hepatic function normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypocalcemia all prolong the QT interval and must be excluded before attributing prolongation to a primary channelopathy.

lab_results.result: Pathogenic variant identified in KCNQ1 (potassium channel IKs subunit), consistent with LQT1 (Romano-Ward syndrome, autosomal dominant).
lab_results.clinical_significance: KCNQ1 loss-of-function mutations cause LQT1, the most common congenital form. LQT1 events are classically triggered by exercise and especially by swimming, matching this patient's presentation.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on a beta-blocker (nadolol), counseled to avoid QT-prolonging medications and competitive swimming, and her family members are referred for cascade genetic testing.

debrief.classic_presentation: LQTS classically presents as syncope, seizures, or sudden cardiac death in a young, otherwise healthy person, often triggered by exertion, emotional stress, or auditory stimuli. A family history of sudden death or unexplained drowning is a major clue. Congenital forms include Romano-Ward (autosomal dominant, no deafness) and Jervell-Lange-Nielsen (autosomal recessive, associated with sensorineural deafness). Acquired forms are triggered by medications (many antiarrhythmics, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, antipsychotics) or electrolyte derangements.
debrief.pathophysiology: LQTS is caused by dysfunction of cardiac ion channels that control repolarization. LQT1 and LQT2 involve loss of function in potassium channels (KCNQ1 encoding IKs and KCNH2/hERG encoding IKr), while LQT3 involves gain-of-function mutations in the SCN5A sodium channel. Delayed repolarization prolongs the action potential and predisposes to early afterdepolarizations, which can trigger torsades de pointes, a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. Torsades can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and cause sudden cardiac death. LQT1 events are often exercise/swimming-triggered, LQT2 events are classically triggered by loud noises, and LQT3 events occur at rest or during sleep.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line therapy is beta-blockers (nadolol or propranolol) to blunt sympathetic triggers. Lifestyle modification includes avoiding competitive sports (especially swimming in LQT1), strenuous emotion, and QT-prolonging drugs. Acute torsades is treated with IV magnesium sulfate; overdrive pacing or isoproterenol can shorten QT in refractory cases. Patients with recurrent syncope despite beta-blockade, documented cardiac arrest, or very high-risk genotypes receive an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD). Family screening is essential.
high_yield_pearls: Romano-Ward = autosomal dominant, no deafness; Jervell-Lange-Nielsen = autosomal recessive with sensorineural deafness.
LQT1 (KCNQ1/IKs) triggers = exercise and swimming; LQT2 (hERG/IKr) = sudden loud noises; LQT3 (SCN5A) = rest/sleep.
Torsades de pointes is treated with IV magnesium sulfate regardless of serum magnesium level.
Common acquired causes include class IA (quinidine) and class III (sotalol) antiarrhythmics, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, antipsychotics (haloperidol), methadone, and ondansetron.
Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypocalcemia all prolong QT and must be corrected.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Cardiovascular chapter, 'Congenital long QT syndrome' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Cardiovascular chapter, 'Torsades de pointes' section.
